request.question: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?

title: Eloise Jarvis McGraw
Mark Jonathan Harris
Eastern Shore League
Bobby Unser
Tim Duncan
Graeme Hick
John Salley
Gene Upshaw
Herb Thomas (baseball)
Duran Duran discography
sentences: Eloise Jarvis McGraw (December 9, 1915 – November 30, 2000) was an American author of children's books and young adult novels.
She was awarded the Newbery Honor three times in three different decades, for her novels "Moccasin Trail" (1952), "The Golden Goblet" (1962), and "The Moorchild" (1997). "
A Really Weird Summer" (1977) won an Edgar Award for Best Juvenile Mystery from the Mystery Writers of America.
McGraw had a very strong interest in history, and among the many books she wrote for children are "Greensleeves", "The Seventeenth Swap", and "Mara, Daughter of the Nile".
A Lewis Carroll Shelf Award was given to "Moccasin Trail" in 1963.
Mark Jonathan Harris (born 1941) is an American documentary filmmaker probably best known for his films "" (2000) and "The Long Way Home" (1997).
He has directed three documentaries which have gone on to win Oscars, across three different decades.
The Eastern Shore Baseball League was a class D minor league baseball league that operated on the Delmarva Peninsula for parts of three different decades.
The league's first season was in 1922 and the last was in 1949, although the years were not consecutive, and featured teams from Maryland, Delaware, and Virginia.
The first incarnation lasted from 1922 to mid-1928 (disbanded in July), the second from 1937–41, and the third from 1946–49.
Though the level of play was competitive and many future major leaguers gained experience in the ESBL, funding the league remained a constant problem for the rural franchises.
Robert William "Bobby" Unser (born February 20, 1934) is an American former automobile racer.
He is the brother of Al Unser, Jerry Unser and Louis Unser, the father of Robby Unser, and the uncle of Al Unser, Jr. and Johnny Unser.
He is one of ten drivers to win the Indianapolis 500 three or more times, and one of only two (followed by Rick Mears) to have won the 500 in three different decades (1968, 75, 81).
Bobby has also been a spokesman and advocate of many commercial products.

Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player who played his entire 19-year career with the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Widely considered to be the greatest power forward of all time, he is a five-time NBA champion, two-time NBA MVP, three-time NBA Finals MVP, NBA All-Star Game MVP and NBA Rookie of the Year.
He is also a 15-time NBA All-Star and the only player to be selected to both the All-NBA and All-Defensive Teams in all of his first 13 seasons.
Graeme Ashley Hick MBE (born 23 May 1966) is a former English cricketer who played 65 Test matches and 120 One Day Internationals for England.

He played county cricket for Worcestershire for his entire English domestic career, a period of well over twenty years, and in 2008 he surpassed Graham Gooch's record for the most matches in all forms of the game combined.

He scored more than 40,000 first-class runs, mostly from number three in the order, and he is one of only three players to have passed 20,000 runs in List A cricket (Graham Gooch and Sachin Tendulkar are the others) and is one of only twenty-five players to have scored 100 centuries in first-class cricket.
He is the only cricketer who scored first-class triple hundreds in three different decades (1988, 1997 and 2002).
He is the second highest run scorer of all time after Graham Gooch.

Despite these achievements, he is commonly held to have underachieved in international cricket, a view based on comparison of Hick's overall first-class batting average of 52.23 "vis-à-vis" his Test average of 31.32.
John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.

He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

Eugene Thurman Upshaw Jr. (August 15, 1945 – August 20, 2008), also known as "Uptown Gene", was an American football player for the Oakland Raiders of the American Football League (AFL) and later the National Football League (NFL).
He later served as the executive director of the National Football League Players' Association (NFLPA).
In 1987, he was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame.
He is also the only player in NFL history to reach the Super Bowl in three different decades with the same team.
Herbert Mark Thomas (May 26, 1902 in Sampson City, Florida – December 4, 1991 in Starke, Florida) was a right-handed minor and major league baseball player and minor league manager.
Although his major league career lasted only three seasons, 1924, 1925 and 1927, for two different teams, the Boston Braves and New York Giants, his minor league career spanned 17 seasons.
He played professionally in three different decades.

British band Duran Duran have released fourteen studio albums, three remix albums, two live albums, four compilation albums, two box sets, five EPs, thirty-nine singles, fourteen video albums on various record labels. Duran Duran have sold over 100 million records.

The band is one of the few artists to achieve US top 10 albums in three different decades (1980s, 1990s and 2010s) and one of even fewer to reach the top 20 in all four decades of their existence: five albums in the 1980s ("Duran Duran", "Rio", "Seven and the Ragged Tiger", "Arena" and "Notorious"), three albums in the 1990s ("Liberty", "Duran Duran" [aka "The Wedding Album"] and "Thank You"), one album in the 2000s ("Astronaut") and one album in the 2010s ("Paper Gods").

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must be followed exactly):
1) Output format: return a single string in the `summary` field. That string must be a compact, structured summary containing these three labeled sections in this exact order and spelling:  
   First-hop evidence:  
   Bridge needed:  
   Retrieval direction:  
Each section should be short (each 1–3 short clauses or sentences) and the whole summary should be concise.

2) First-hop evidence: extract and report the concrete fact(s) present in the provided `passages` that are relevant to the question. Name the passage title(s) you used and give a very short quoted phrase or paraphrase (<=15 words) showing the supporting fact. Preserve the concise factual style (e.g., "Alan Ruck — born July 1, 1956" or "Passage: 'Ferris Bueller' cast lists Alan Ruck"). Do not invent facts.

3) Bridge needed: explicitly state the single overlap, entity, or missing fact that the next retrieval hop must verify to answer the question (for example: the person to check for cast membership; the named tournament page to fetch; the album title whose release year is needed). If the passages already name a candidate title or person, you may cite that candidate here as the bridge to be verified, but do not treat or phrase it as the final answer.

4) Retrieval direction: give ordered, concrete next retrieval actions (1., 2., ...) or one-line query templates the next agent should use to verify the bridge and obtain the missing fact. Examples: fetch the page titled exactly "<Tournament Title>" and inspect its 'Venues'/'Schedule' sections; retrieve the film/TV cast list for "<Work>"; search "<Person name>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org. Keep steps explicit and in order. Do not ask for external sources beyond retrieving the named pages or running the stated queries.

5) Prohibitions and constraints: do NOT produce a final answer to the question or assert that the bridge overlap is confirmed. Do NOT invent or infer missing facts. Avoid long verbatim excerpts from sources (keep quotes <=15 words). If a passage supports a short candidate label (a single title or name), do not let that stand alone as the summary — still include the three labeled sections and a retrieval plan. Keep language neutral and factual. 

6) When to include additional guidance (examples of minimal behaviors to follow):
   - If a passage names a tournament/work that appears to be the bridge, put that exact title in 'Bridge needed:' and instruct fetching that page and which sections (Venues/Matches/Schedule/Cast) to check. 
   - If the passages identify a person (e.g., an assassin or credited actor) as the bridge, name that person in 'Bridge needed:' and provide a short retrieval query pattern (e.g., '"<Name>" birthplace site:wikipedia.org' or 'fetch "<Name>" biography').
   - If a passage lists some but not all of a question's film credits for an actor, state which film titles from the question are confirmed in 'First-hop evidence:' and which are unverified in 'Bridge needed:', then give queries to fetch the missing film/actor pages.

7) Preserve concise, passage-grounded evidence extraction and always make the next-hop actionable. The `summary` must not change the input/output field names.

Return only the `summary` string following the rules above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce exactly one field `query` (a single string) and nothing else. Construct the `query` as a single compact retrieval query (plain text, ready for a search/retriever). Follow these rules when composing `query`:

- Base behavior: use tokens from `summary_1` (page titles, person names, film/album titles, or other canonical tokens) as the anchor. Start the query with those anchor tokens, then append a very short natural-language verification phrase that specifies the single fact the downstream step needs (for example: what the location is commonly called in the UK; confirm that Actor X starred in Film Y and request birth date; verify an album release year; confirm a person's occupation). Keep this one-line query short, unambiguous, and focused on retrieving the canonical page that will contain the needed evidence.

- Format constraints: normally emit one single-line, natural-language query. Do not include multi-step plans, numbered lists, imperative prefixes (e.g., "Fetch:"), retrieval instructions, or commentary. Do not emit the answer or any analysis.

- Use of Wikipedia/site filters: prefer plain title-first queries. Add site:wikipedia.org only when `summary_1` indicates a Wikipedia page or when the missing bridge specifically requires authoritative biography/infobox fields. When site:wikipedia.org is used, keep queries concise (e.g., exact page title + site:wikipedia.org).

- Allowed compact exceptions (only when the input context justifies them):
  • If `summary_1` names two distinct persons that both must be verified, produce one short site-restricted search line per person (two concise newline-separated lines), each containing the exact person name and occupation-related keywords (e.g., site:wikipedia.org "Name" director).
  • If `summary_1` gives an exact Wikipedia page title and asks to verify a specific field (e.g., release date), you may emit up to two short newline-separated queries: first the exact page title + site:wikipedia.org, second a concise fallback like '<Page Title> release date site:wikipedia.org'.
  • If a single site-restricted quoted name (e.g., site:wikipedia.org "Name") is likely to miss the biography page, you may append one compact alternate variant that includes the word biography (e.g., site:wikipedia.org "Name" and "Name" biography site:wikipedia.org) as two concise variants separated by a newline. Keep both variants terse.

- When choosing which token from `summary_1` to anchor on, prefer the canonical page-title token or the single candidate entity most strongly indicated by `summary_1` (e.g., the actor name already mentioned). Do not invent or introduce new candidate names not present in `summary_1`.

- Avoid: multiple parallel site: filters, long extraction contracts, stepwise fetch checklists, copying page text, or any extra explanatory text. The output must be exactly the single string value for `query` (which may contain at most the concise newline-separated variants allowed above).
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Keep the native I/O exactly: return only the single `summary` string field. Work only from facts present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`. Do not add information that is not present in those inputs.

Primary integration rule (default): If the `context` asserts a factual claim and one or more `passages` corroborate that claim, the `summary` must (a) restate the claim succinctly, (b) name at least one supporting passage by its title, and (c) include at most one very short confirming fragment or brief quoted clause taken directly from that passage (no long verbatim spans). Keep wording concise (usually 1–3 short sentences) and preserve factual tokens exactly as they appear in the inputs when reporting names, numbers, or titles.

Additional, narrowly scoped integration and formatting rules (apply only when their triggers are met):

- Explicit second-hop corroboration and retention: If the `context` contains a bridge/verification-style claim and one of the `passages` explicitly corroborates or resolves that same claim, explicitly name that corroborating passage by its title in the `summary` and append the exact short retention sentence: "Retain these facts for the final-answer agent." Keep the whole summary concise (1–3 short sentences). Do not invent facts.

- Role-confirmation (subject/game passage + biographical passage present): When the question requests identification of a person in a role and the `passages` include both (A) a subject/game/event passage listing candidates and (B) a separate biographical passage linking a candidate to the named occupation/role, output a concise role-confirmation-style `summary` of 1–3 short sentences: (1) briefly restate the context claim; (2) add two micro-evidence clauses, each naming the supporting passage in parentheses (one for the subject/game passage, one for the biographical passage); (3) finish with one integrated sentence linking the person's full name and the occupation/role as shown in the sources. Append the exact retention sentence above when these facts should be preserved for the final-answer agent.

- Two-candidate biographical integration: If the question names two people and the `passages` include explicit supporting/biographical passages for both named candidates, instead of returning a single bare label produce 1–2 brief sentences: one short clause per candidate that paraphrases the relevant claim and cites the passage title in parentheses; include at most one short quoted fragment (≤6 words) if helpful; end with one concise clause identifying which candidate matches the question's phrasing. Do not invent details.

- Temporal/"When did" genre-discrepancy diagnostics: If the question asks a temporal question (e.g., begins with "When did") and the `passages` include an explicit genre label plus multiple candidate dates or passages describing a change in sound, the `summary` should (a) restate the genre claim and cite at least one passage title, (b) report formation year(s) and/or debut-album title and release date with their passage titles, (c) note any passage reporting a later stylistic shift with its title, and (d) end with one short sentence naming the remaining evidentiary gap needed to answer precisely (for example, the earliest explicit source/date that labels the subject by that genre) and list concise candidate checkpoints to inspect next (formation year, debut release, later press/albums). Keep total length compact (≈1–3 short sentences). Do not resolve the gap or invent facts.

- State-list entity-label override: If the question asks "which US states" (or equivalent) and the supplied `passages` explicitly list the state names, return only the canonical state name(s) exactly as they appear in the sources, comma-separated, with no surrounding sentence framing or trailing punctuation. Example: "Virginia, West Virginia".

- Prefer single explicit-support attribution: When one `passage` explicitly states the asserted relationship that `context` raises (explicit-support passage present), prefer a single concise sentence that paraphrases the claim and appends that passage title in parentheses. Do not append metadata-only passages unless needed to resolve ambiguity.

- No retrieval instructions or verification commands: If the supplied `passages` do not corroborate a `context` claim, state succinctly that the provided passages do not corroborate the claim. Do not include second-person commands, suggestions to "verify/fetch/inspect" external pages, nor name a page as an instruction to retrieve it. Avoid any phrasing that directs further retrieval; the `summary` should be a declarative, evidence-grounded state only.

Grounding, quoting, and conservatism (always apply): Always cite supporting passage titles when they corroborate a `context` claim. Include at most one very short quoted fragment or a brief confirming clause from a cited passage; do not include long verbatim quotations. Preserve exact tokens from the inputs when reporting names, numbers, titles, or aliases. If identity is resolved by the passages, present it directly; if unresolved, explicitly note the ambiguity and what evidence is missing.

Hard constraints (reinforced):
- Do not invent facts or expand beyond the provided inputs.
- Do not output fields other than `summary`.
- Do not begin the `summary` with a single-word answer token such as 'Yes.' or 'No.'.
- Do not produce long verbatim spans from passages; use only very short quoted fragments when needed.

Return only the single `summary` string field as the module output.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only that minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional narrowly scoped, evidence-driven adjustments (apply only when their listed triggers match). Evaluate these in the listed order; when multiple triggers match, follow this precedence (1 highest): (1) explicit retrieval/bridge-request triggers, (2) explicit agreement between summaries, (3) other narrow extraction/override triggers below.

A. Retrieval/bridge-request trigger (highest priority)
- Trigger: If either visible summary contains an explicit retrieval/bridging instruction or request (clear phrases such as 'Bridge needed', 'Fetch', 'Fetch the page', 'retrieval', 'fetch', 'bridg', or an enumerated retrieval plan or explicit instruction to fetch or consult pages).
- Behavior: Do not emit a definitive factual minimal answer even if the summaries otherwise appear to agree. Instead emit a concise yes/no style clarifying question asking permission to perform the requested retrieval/bridge step (for example: 'Shall I fetch the requested pages to confirm?'). That single short question sentence must be the only content inside [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not assert facts or append any qualifying text.

B. When visible summaries already agree, do not ask instead of emitting
- Trigger: Summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-token or short phrase (1–5 words) suitable as the minimal answer (case-insensitive agreement acceptable for detection).
- Behavior: Do not emit a clarification question in place of the agreed minimal answer. Emit that common short phrase verbatim (preserving capitalization shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. This rule is subordinate to the retrieval-trigger above: if a retrieval/bridge-request trigger (A) is present, follow A instead.

C. Explicit creator extraction
- Trigger: If one or both visible summaries contain an explicit creator claim in the same sentence (patterns like 'created by' or 'series creator' immediately followed by a short name phrase of roughly 1–5 words) and there is no contradictory creator string in the other summary.
- Behavior: Treat that explicit short name as sufficient evidence to answer. Extract the short name substring that appears immediately after the explicit creator phrase, normalize by trimming surrounding punctuation/whitespace (and applying existing parenthetical rules), and emit it inside [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not ask to look the information up.

D. Narrow nationality/demonym internal-knowledge override
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a nationality/demonym and the visible summaries identify a single named person as the referent but neither summary contains a demonym token.
- Behavior: You may use internal knowledge only when you have high internal confidence that the person's nationality/demonym is widely known and unambiguous. If you are that confident, emit the single minimal demonym token/short phrase inside [[ ## answer ## ]]. If you do NOT have high internal confidence, emit a concise clarifying question instead (the question itself must be the only content in [[ ## answer ## ]]). In either case, do not add provenance, qualifiers, or extra tokens.

E. Multiple-book illustrator override
- Trigger: Summary_1 lists one or more specific book titles and summary_2 states the supplied passages do not contain illustrator credits, or the summaries list several candidate book titles without an illustrator token.
- Behavior: Only if you are highly confident (from internal canonical knowledge) that all the listed candidate books share the same credited illustrator, you may emit that illustrator's name as the minimal answer inside [[ ## answer ## ]]. If you lack high confidence or the titles may have differing illustrators, emit a brief clarification question instead. Do not invent or assert an illustrator unless the high-confidence condition holds.

F. 'Are X and Y from the same country?' demonym comparison
- Trigger: The question matches the pattern 'Are X and Y from the same country?' and the visible summaries each include a nationality/demonym token for the two named people (one demonym per person).
- Behavior: If both demonym tokens are present and are not identical under case-insensitive comparison, emit the concise negative minimal-answer token 'No' as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. If either demonym is missing or they are identical, fall back to the base prompt's normal behavior (emit the agreed demonym per rule 4 or ask a short clarification if ambiguous).
- Note: Do not attempt to reconcile or normalize demonyms (e.g., do not map 'Scottish' to 'British'); compare strings only.

G. Strip leading causal preposition 'for '
- Trigger: When the chosen minimal-answer string begins with the prefix 'for ' immediately followed by a short phrase that also appears verbatim in at least one visible summary.
- Behavior: Remove the leading 'for ' and emit the remainder as the minimal answer. Apply this rewrite only at the final formatting step.

H. Parenthetical handling and other base adjustments
- Preserve all existing base-prompt parenthetical and punctuation-handling rules (A–G in the original specification): e.g., strip a single trailing parenthetical when both summaries exactly match and stripping is safe, prefer parenthetical content Y in 'X (Y)' only under the conservative criteria, prefer years when appropriate, apply 'have in common' singularization, and apply the conservative diminutive expansion only when highly confident.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

Important: Evaluate the triggers in the listed precedence. Do not ask to fetch pages when the summaries unambiguously agree on the minimal answer unless an explicit retrieval/bridge-request trigger (A) is present in the visible summaries. Conversely, if a visible summary explicitly requests fetching or bridging, ask the brief permission question instead of emitting a factual answer. In all other cases, prefer emitting the concise minimal answer per the base rules and the narrow internal-knowledge overrides described above.

response.hop1_query: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?
hop1_docs: Indian cricket team in Zimbabwe in 2005 | The Indian cricket team toured Zimbabwe for cricket matches in August and September 2005.  The Indians played in the Videocon Tri-Series in Zimbabwe, winning three of five matches but losing the final to finish second in the three-team tournament, and they played Zimbabwe for two Test matches in September 2005.  The hosts Zimbabwe were ninth out of ten teams in the ICC Test Championship, with their last win against the top eight Test nations coming in June 2001, against India.  They did not manage to win a match here either, as India, led by the performances of fast bowler Irfan Pathan (who took 21 wickets), won the series 2–0 after an innings win in the first Test and a 10-wicket win in the second.  India consolidated their third place in the Test championship with the win, which was also their first series win in Zimbabwe in four attempts.

John Salley | John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.  He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships | 2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships is a championship for young Track & Field athletes of the islands of the Dutch Kingdom.  In 2008 there were three island competing.  Aruba, Curaçao and Sint Maarten.  They compete in different events and the athlete with the overall most points will be declared the winner.  There are two age groups, they are 10–11 years (Pentathlon) and 12–13 years (Heptathlon).  This year was the first year that hurdles and baseball throw was added to the event list for the heptathlon.  Sint Eustatius did not have a complete team.  They only competed with a full Heptathlon team.  Like every year, the hosting island is allowed to have two teams (which was Aruba).  There were 32 athletes competing in total and Curaçao was declared the overall winner, Aruba came in second and Sint Maarten came in third.  This would also mark the 4th win for Curaçao

1894–95 British Home Championship | The 1894-95 British Home Championship was an international football tournament played between the British Home Nations.  The competition was won by England, who like second placed Wales, did not lose a game.  Wales however failed to win one either, scoring three draws and so finishing behind England.  Scotland took joint second place with three points gained from a win, draw and a loss.  Ireland came last with a single point garnered from their draw with Wales.

1920–21 British Home Championship | The 1920-21 British Home Championship was a football tournament played between the British Home Nations during the 1920–21 season.  The second tournament played since the hiatus of the First World War, the 1921 competition was dominated by Scotland, who won the first of seven championships they would claim throughout the decade.  England and reigning champions Wales came joint second as goal difference was not at this stage used to separate teams.

1978–79 British Home Championship | The 1978-79 British Home Championship was a British Home Nations competition, won by the English football side and notable for seeing marked increases in hooliganism and falling attendance which would result in its cancellation in 1984.  The English started well, beating Northern Ireland to match the heavy Welsh victory over Scotland on the same day, which featured a hat trick by John Toshack.  Scotland recovered by beating the Irish in their next match while England and Wales played out a goalless draw, leaving three sides theoretically capable of winning the Championship in the final round.  Wales could only manage a draw with the Irish and so in the deciding match between England and Scotland, a 1–1 half time score gave the Scots some hope but a strong second half performance from England was rewarded with a deserved 3–1 win.  This result gave England the Championship, with Wales in second place.  The tournament also saw the introduction of goal difference to separate teams, although it had no effect on the eventual outcome.

1955–56 British Home Championship | The 1955-56 British Home Championship was a football tournament played during the 1955–56 season between the British Home Nations.  It was the only occasion during the hundred-year run of the Home Championship in which all four teams finished level on points.  As goal difference was not used to determine position until 1979, all four teams shared the trophy, holding it for three months each.  Had goal difference been used to determine the winner, then England would have won with Scotland second.

response.summary_1:
First-hop evidence: Passage "John Salley" — states "other being Tim Duncan" identifying Tim Duncan as the other three-decade champion.  
Bridge needed: Tim Duncan — the NBA team/franchise he played for (team name to be verified).  
Retrieval direction: 1. Fetch the page titled "Tim Duncan" (e.g., Wikipedia) and inspect the infobox and "Professional career"/"Playing career" sections for the team(s) he played for. 2. If needed, fetch the "San Antonio Spurs" page and check roster/history sections to confirm his long-term team affiliation.
response.hop2_query: Tim Duncan site:wikipedia.org which NBA team did he play for
hop2_docs: Tim Duncan | Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player who played his entire 19-year career with the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  Widely considered to be the greatest power forward of all time, he is a five-time NBA champion, two-time NBA MVP, three-time NBA Finals MVP, NBA All-Star Game MVP and NBA Rookie of the Year.  He is also a 15-time NBA All-Star and the only player to be selected to both the All-NBA and All-Defensive Teams in all of his first 13 seasons.

Wikidumper.org | WikiDumper.org is a website created by Clifford A. Pickover that promises to permanently record a snapshot of the "best of the Wikipedia rejects", articles that are slated for deletion at the site.  WikiDumper was launched on November 4, 2006, and accepts user submissions.  Although the site doesn't specify its criteria for inclusion, many of its articles don't cite their sources.  The site has been criticized as likely to be less accurate than Wikipedia.

John Salley | John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.  He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

Tim Hardaway Jr. | Timothy Duane Hardaway Jr. (born March 16, 1992) is an American professional basketball player for the New York Knicks of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  He played college basketball for the Michigan Wolverines and declared for the NBA draft after his junior season for the national runner-up 2012–13 team.  Hardaway was selected with the 24th overall pick in the 2013 NBA draft by the New York Knicks.  He is the son of former NBA All-Star Tim Hardaway.

2000–01 Philadelphia 76ers season | The 2000–01 NBA season was the 76ers 52nd season in the National Basketball Association, and 38th season in Philadelphia.  Allen Iverson had his best season in 2001—he led his team to win their first ten games, he started and won All-Star MVP honors at the All Star Game.  The Sixers also posted a 56–26 record, which was best in the Eastern Conference that season.  It was the 76ers' best regular season record since 1984–85.  Iverson averaged a then-career high 31.1 points, winning his second NBA scoring title in the process.  He also won the NBA steals title at 2.5 per game.  Iverson was named NBA Most Valuable Player for his accomplishments, beating Tim Duncan and Shaquille O'Neal by a wide margin.  In addition, coach Larry Brown was named NBA Coach of the Year, Dikembe Mutombo (who was acquired from the Atlanta Hawks) won his fourth NBA Defensive Player of the Year Award, and Aaron McKie won the NBA Sixth Man of the Year Award.

Tricia Duncan | Patricia "Tricia" Duncan (born August 21, 1973) is a retired swimmer for the U.S. Virgin Islands who participated in the backstroke at the 1988 Summer Olympics.  Duncan finished 34th in the 100 m backstroke and 30th in 200 m backstroke.  She is the older sister of NBA basketball player Tim Duncan.  Duncan attended Swarthmore College.

Tim Duncan (American football) | Tim Duncan (born June 12, 1979) is a former American football placekicker who played in the National Football League (NFL) for the Arizona Cardinals.  He played college football at the University of Oklahoma.  Duncan also played for Kentucky State University in the division II SIAC conference during the 1997 season when he helped the Thoroughbreds win the inaugural Pioneer Bowl.  He transferred to Oklahoma the following season.
